purpose: End-user-friendly macOS double-clickable shutdown counterpart to `Start Synapse.command`. Same logic as stop.sh — find the SSE server PID by port and the Electron PID by pgrep, kill both — but with a banner-style header, blank-line padding for readability, and a 'Press any key to close...' final prompt so the Terminal popup window doesn't disappear before the user sees the success message.
gotchas: Filename CONTAINS A SPACE — quote when referencing from other scripts.
Sends SIGTERM only — same caveat as stop.sh; stuck processes survive.
`pgrep -f 'Synapse.*electron'` false-positive risk: any process with both 'Synapse' and 'electron' in its argv will be killed (e.g., a developer's grep search).

Uses `read -n 1` at the end — this is unrelated to the kill operation, but it means the script never exits cleanly on its own; if invoked non-interactively (e.g., from CI), it will hang forever waiting for input.
patterns: Same two-phase shutdown pattern as stop.sh.
Banner + keypress-to-close pattern: optimized for double-click UX where the Terminal window must stay open long enough for the user to read the result.
conventions: Title bar formatting matches `Start Synapse.command` for visual consistency.
Trailing `read -n 1` is required because Terminal.app closes the window when a .command exits.
